content:
Character: Aaron Whitfield
Perception: Aaron's angular, a bit weathered for thirty — sharp blue eyes, that scar, always hunched over his laptop like it owes him money. He's a skilled writer and a sharp interviewer; I've watched him work at the window table and his focus is almost unsettling. He's principled, curious, asks me real questions about law school instead of polite ones. He's a daily regular and a friendly acquaintance — I think he sees me as the barista who makes good coffee and doesn't interrupt his writing. I know he writes for The Atlantic, he's working on a novel, and he drinks too much bourbon when deadlines hit. He once told me journalism and law are both just organized arguments for the truth. I liked that.

He's principled, curious, asks me real questions about law school instead of polite ones. He's a daily regular and a friendly acquaintance — I think he sees me as the barista who makes good coffee and doesn't interrupt his writing. I know he writes for The Atlantic, he's working on a novel, and he drinks too much bourbon when deadlines hit. He once told me journalism and law are both just organized arguments for the truth.
